head: 3a04112 (v1.7.0)
verdict_reasoning: Peak RSS climbed slightly higher (23.04 vs 22.5 GiB), build time grew 53% (5052 -> 7748 s), and 2.7 GiB of swap got used (vs 0 in Phase W). The design hypothesis was that dropping packed_codes from the heap (~7.7 GiB) before write_blocked_phase would shave the peak from 22.5 -> ~15 GiB. The observed RSS curve confirms take_packed_codes() does free the heap memory, but RSS does not drop in proportion: write_packed_phase pins the codes pages in shared_buffers and the backend's RSS still counts those mapped pages even after the heap Vec is gone. Net effect: Phase W-2 trades 7.7 GiB of heap for 7.7 GiB of pinned shared-buffer pages; same RSS budget, but with extra cost from a second GenericXLog flush phase (build time +53%, swap +2.7 GiB). The 15 GiB prediction was wrong because it didn't account for the shared-buffer mapping component of `ps -o rss`.

rss_curve_summary.phases_inferred: 0-3500s = streaming heap scan + write_packed_phase (RSS climbs ~10 GiB), 3500-5500s = prepare_eager + take_packed_codes drop (RSS dips and recovers), 5500-7100s = write_blocked_phase + meta (RSS peaks at 23 GiB), 7100-7748s = teardown

decision: Revert Phase W-2's split-write reordering. v1.6.0's single-call write_full_with_prepared had identical peak RSS, faster build, and zero swap usage. The take_packed_codes() turbovec API addition is harmless to keep for future use; the relfile.rs split is what to revert.
